patches.find: which does not match the 2010 date cited by the dome. If no peer-reviewed publication exists under the cited reference, the dome is relying on an unverifiable source for this WIN.
patches.replace: which does not match the 2010 date cited by the dome (we cite the attribution throughout as 'Sun et al., attributed 2010; closest verified English-language record: doi:10.6038/cjg20130306, published 2013'). If no peer-reviewed publication exists under the cited reference, the dome is relying on an unverifiable source for this WIN.
patches.rationale: ISS-1337: Curmudgeon c3 hole 3 — date mismatch between dome's '2010' citation and closest verifiable English-language paper (2013). Current text notes the discrepancy but does not show how we handle it in our own citations. Adding a parenthetical that shows our internal treatment ('attributed 2010; closest verified: 2013') makes the qualification transparent and prevents a dome defender from claiming we silently accept the 2010 date.

patches.find: The 2009 China SG campaign — continuously sampled across C1/C4 — would have captured a 10–13 µGal contact-phase pulse if one existed. It saw none.
patches.replace: The 2009 China SG campaign — continuously sampled across C1/C4 — would have captured a 10–13 µGal contact-phase pulse if one existed. Superconducting gravimeters of this generation have raw noise floors below 1 µGal at minute timescales (Van Camp, 2005; Crossley et al., 2013), so a 10–13 µGal pulse would appear even without Level-3 processing. It saw none.
patches.rationale: ISS-1336: Curmudgeon c3 hole 2 — 'would have captured' claim assumes the 2009 Chinese SG methodology matches PRED-TIER-3 Level-3 processing. Argument is stronger if grounded in the SG noise floor independently: Van Camp (2005) and Crossley et al. (2013) document raw SG noise floors <1 µGal at minute timescales, meaning a 10-13 µGal signal would be visible without any specific processing pipeline.

patches.find: superconducting gravimeters have ~1,000× the sensitivity of the Mohe spring gravimeter. If the signal exists, SGs would see it.
patches.replace: superconducting gravimeters of this generation typically have noise floors of 0.1–1 µGal at minute timescales — roughly 1,000× more precise than a LaCoste-Romberg spring gravimeter. If the signal exists, SGs would see it.
patches.rationale: ISS-1339: Curmudgeon c3 — the ~1,000× ratio was drawn from WIN-013 context (Van Camp 2001, European SG vs. Mohe spring), applied here to the Chinese SG network without Chinese-specific specs. Replacing with a generic generation-level noise-floor range (0.1–1 µGal; LaCoste-Romberg reference) makes the claim self-standing without requiring the specific European SG specs to also apply to the Chinese SG network.

patches.find: the distinction between 'electromagnetic' and 'gravitational' mechanisms for a gravimeter measurement is physically meaningless.
patches.replace: the dome's own WIN-011 positive-signal claim is based on Mohe spring-gravimeter data; if a −6.5 µGal coupling-mediated signal exists at eclipse contacts, both spring and superconducting instruments should detect it at their respective noise floors regardless of whether the mechanism is labelled electromagnetic or gravitational.
patches.rationale: ISS-1349: Curmudgeon c3 hole 7 — 'physically meaningless' overstates. Instruments do differ in susceptibility to non-gravitational noise. Replaced with a more precise argument: the dome's own WIN-011 denominator (the −6.5 µGal Mohe spring reading) must be real if kappa is real, and if it is real, superior instruments (SGs, 1000× more precise) should also detect it. This is a stronger and more defensible framing than claiming the mechanism distinction is 'meaningless.'

patches.find: dome model's −6.5 μGal comes from this study but misrepresents its timing: gravitational shielding would peak at maximum eclipse (maximum coverage), not at the contacts.
patches.replace: dome model's −6.5 μGal comes from this study. Even granting the dome its preferred value for the sake of argument, the reported timing contradicts the shielding interpretation: the Wang 2000 paper recorded the anomaly at first and last contact — not at eclipse maximum. Gravitational shielding would peak at maximum coverage, not at the contacts.
patches.rationale: ISS-1280: Companion fix to EXP-192 (applied to WIN-012). WIN-012 now uses 'even granting the dome its preferred numerical value for the sake of argument' framing (ISS-1212). WIN-011 should be consistent: add the 'even granting' charitability frame and clarify that the timing observation comes from Wang 2000 itself (the contacts anomaly is their own reported result), while our critique is that the dome's shielding interpretation cannot explain why the signal peaks at contacts rather than maximum.
